Module 1 > Introduction to Financial Statements

1-3A – Basic Financial Statements
Sherry’s Shuttles is a bus company offering rides to outdoor adventurers. In the summer, she caters to mountain
bikers and in winter to skiers. Sherry’s company has the following account balances, all on December 31, 2029
and for the year then ended unless otherwise noted:
Buildings, net $100,000 Fuel expense $10,000
Wages expense 35,000 Dividends 5,000
Shuttle revenue 140,000 Equipment, net 50,000
Bank loan payable 30,000 Common shares 30,000
Accounts receivable 12,000 Accounts payable 17,000
Office supplies 3,000 Insurance expense 12,000
Utilities expense 14,000 Depreciation expense 21,000
Repairs expense 13,000 Cash 25,000
Wages payable 3,000 Retained earnings (January 1, 2029) 80,000
Required:
a.) Prepare an income statement for the year ended December 31, 2029.
b.) Prepare a statement of retained earnings for the year ended December 31, 2029.
c.) Prepare a balance sheet as at December 31, 2029.
d.) Based on your financial statements, compute:
i. The current ratio
ii. The debt ratio
iii. The equity ratio

1-3B – Basic Financial Statements
John’s Jungle Gyms is an indoor play area for children. Parents bring their toddlers to play on germ-covered
slides, ladders, ball pits, monkey bars and other pieces of equipment. The following account balances relate to
the company’s October 31, 2029 year-end financial statements:

Buildings, net 225,000 Repair expense 20,000
Accounts payable 4,000 Admission revenue 240,000
Mortgage payable 150,000 Dividends 30,000
Wages expense 90,000 Cash 4,000
Retained earnings (November 1, 2028) 93,000 Accounts receivable 7,000
Utilities expense 5,000 Wages payable 2,000
Equipment, net 74,000 Insurance expense 10,000
There were no common shares issued or repurchased during the month.
Required:
a.) Prepare an income statement for the year ended October 31, 2029.
b.) Prepare a statement of retained earnings for the year ended October 31, 2029.
c.) Prepare a balance sheet as at October 31, 2029.
d.) Based on your financial statements, compute:
i. The current ratio
ii. The debt ratio
iii. The equity ratio

1-4A – More Complex Financial Statements
Industrial Relations Ltd. offers mediation services for unionized companies. When there is a contract dispute
between a union and company management, often the two sides require an outside consultant to assist in the
negotiations – Industrial Relations Ltd. fills this role. The following account balances relate to the company’s
June 30, 2029 year-end financial statements:
Short-term notes payable $10,000 Dividends $50,000
Accounts receivable 11,000 Income tax expense 80,000
Accumulated depreciation - equipment 15,000 Wages expense 405,000
Interest expense 40,000 Common shares 1,000
Equipment 110,000 Retained earnings (July 1, 2028) 374,000
Depreciation expense 30,000 Buildings 900,000
Supplies 6,000 Accounts payable 5,000
Accumulated depreciation - buildings 125,000 Cash 53,000
Consulting revenue 810,000 Mortgage payable 450,000
Travel expense 70,000 Supplies expense 35,000
Notes:
• There were no common shares issued or repurchased during the year.
• The current portion of the mortgage payable was $20,000.
Required:
a.) Prepare an income statement for the year ended June 30, 2029.
b.) Prepare a statement of retained earnings for the year ended June 30, 2029.
c.) Prepare a balance sheet as at June 30, 2029.
d.) Based on your financial statements, compute:
i. The current ratio
ii. The debt ratio
iii. The equity ratio

2-1A – Basic Journal Entries – New Company
Fred McCarthy started his company, Cheapo Tours to take customers to the Grand Canyon from Las Vegas.
The company began operations in March. The following transactions occurred during the company’s first
month:
March 1 Fred invested $10,000 cash and a van valued at $35,000 in exchange for 1,000 common shares.
March 3 Paid $1,000 cash to advertise online.
March 5 Purchased equipment on account: $3,000.
March 6 Purchased a second van for $28,000. Paid $5,000 and took the rest as a car loan.
March 15 Took first tour group to see the Grand Canyon. The trip was a success. Customers paid $1,000
each for their tour. In total, thirteen customers on the tour paid $12,000. One customer was not
able to pay, but promised to pay his $1,000 by the end of the month.
March 16 Joe paid his employees’ salaries of $3,000.
March 17 Purchased fuel for the vehicles: $500 cash
March 19 Paid $800 to repair a broken window on one of the vans.
March 20 Paid for the March 5 equipment purchase.
March 22 Received a utilities bill: $200. Did not pay yet.
March 25 Received the last $1,000 from the March 15 tour.
March 31 Took a second tour group of 15 people. Each paid $1,000.
March 31 Paid employees’ salaries of $3,000.
March 31 Shareholders took a cash dividend of $5,000.
Required:
Record all necessary journal entries based on the transactions above.

2-1B – Basic Journal Entries – New Company
Joe’s Car Repairs started operating on June 1. The following transactions occurred during June:
June 1 Joe invested $25,000 cash and invested equipment valued at $15,000 in exchange for 5,000
common shares.
June 3 Purchased equipment on account. $12,000 is due to be paid on July 3.
June 6 Paid $300 to sponsor a local sports team.
June 14 Paid employees’ salaries of $4,000.
June 15 Performed car repair work for the first two weeks of June. Billed and received $7,000.
June 16 Performed car repair work for customer #233 - $2,000. The customer did not pay but agreed to
pay within 30 days.
June 22 Paid for the equipment purchase from June 3.
June 26 Received one half of the amount owed from the June 16 transaction.
June 28 Paid $250 cash dividend to shareholders.
June 30 Paid employees’ salaries of $4,000.
June 30 Received a telephone bill for $125 for June. Not yet paid.
Required:
Record all necessary journal entries based on the transactions above.

3-1A – 5 types of Adjustments
This problem explores 5 common types of adjusting journal entries. All problems relate to ABC Company, which has a
fiscal year-end of December 31.
a.) Prepaid expenses - Insurance
ABC Company purchases a one-year insurance policy on June 1, 2029 for $1,800 cash.
Required
Record the entry for the purchase of insurance and for the year-end adjustment.
b.) Depreciation
ABC Company purchases a vehicle on August 31, 2029 for $36,000 cash. The vehicle is expected to be useful for ten
years, after which time it will have no residual value. The company wishes to use straight-line depreciation.
Required
Record the journal entry for the purchase of the vehicle and for the year-end adjustment.
c.) Accrued expenses - Interest
On May 1, 2029, ABC Company borrows $10,000 and signs a note payable. The debt carries annual interest of 6%
and is repaid in full (with interest) on July 1, 2030.
Required
Record the journal entry for the initial borrowing, the year-end adjustment and the repayment of the debt.
d.) Accrued revenues
On December 15, 2029, ABC Company agreed to provide consulting services to a client at a rate of $3,000 per month,
with the first payment expected on January 15th, 2030. As of December 31, 2029, the company had done good work, and
the client was pleased. ABC Company completed the contract on January 15th, and received the $3,000 payment as
agreed.
Required
Record the journal entry for the year-end adjustment, and for the payment on January 15th.
e.) Unearned revenues
A client pre-pays ABC Company on November 1, 2029 for five months of consulting service (from November
through the end of March). The company pays $15,000. ABC Company earns the money evenly over the life of the
project, and has fulfilled its obligations up to December 31.
Required
Record the journal entry November 1 and for the year-end adjustment.

3-1B – 5 types of Adjustments
This problem explores 5 common types of adjusting journal entries. All problems relate to XYZ Company, which has a
fiscal year-end of December 31.
a.) Prepaid expenses - Supplies
On December 15, XYZ Company was completely out of supplies, so the company purchased $1,500 of supplies
on account. On December 31, a supply count revealed $200 of supplies were on hand.
Required
Record the entry for the purchase of supplies and for the year-end adjustment.
b.) Depreciation
On March 31, 2029, XYZ Company purchases a computer for $1,500 cash. The computer is expected to be useful for
5 years, after which time it will have no residual value. The company wishes to use straight-line depreciation.
Required
Record the journal entry for the purchase of the computer and for the year-end adjustment. What is the net book value of
the computer on December 31, 2029?
c.) Accrued expenses - Salaries
XYZ Company’s employees work Monday to Friday (not weekends), and the company pays total
salaries of $20,000 on every second Thursday – see calendar below:
Required
Record the adjusting journal entry for December 31 and for the salary payment on January 3.
d.) Accrued revenues - interest
On October 1, 2029, XYZ Company loaned an employee (Fred Smith) $1,000. Fred signed a note promising to pay
back the $1,000 in full plus interest at annual rate of 12%. On May 1, Fred pays back the note and accrued interest.
Required
Record the journal entry for the initial loan, the year-end adjustment and the repayment.
e.) Unearned revenues
On December 1, 2029, three customers each prepaid $500 for a 5-month consulting package. XYZ Company fulfilled
its obligations up to December 31.
Required
Record the journal entry December 1 and for the year-end adjustment.

3-2A – Recording transactions and adjusting entries
Iginla Inc. has a fiscal year end of December 31, 2029. Below are transactions that occurred during the year:
a.) On February 1, the company purchased a 1-year insurance policy for $4,800 cash.
b.) On October 17, the company had no supplies, so it purchased $2,000 of supplies on account. The
supplies were counted at year end, and there were $450 remaining.
c.) On August 31, the company purchased a truck for $36,000 cash. The truck’s estimated useful life is
12 years, and there is no expected residual value. The company uses straight-line depreciation.
d.) On September 30, the company signed a note payable, borrowing $10,000 cash from a local credit
union at an annual interest rate of 7%. They promised to repay $10,000 plus interest on May 1,
2030.
e.) On November 1, the company loaned $1,000 cash to an employee. The employee promised to repay
the company the principal plus 3% annual interest on January 31, 2030.
f.) On November 20, the company received a $5,000 advance payment for cleaning services it would
deliver for the months of December and January. As of December 31, it had successfully fulfilled its
first month of obligation.
Required:
For the transactions above, record a journal entry for the original transaction and record the required year-end
adjustment. (If no journal entry is required, write “no entry”.)

would be delivering over the next four months. As of fiscal year end, the first three months of the
service had been delivered on time and on schedule with one more month remaining.
Required:

adjustment. (If no journal entry is required, write “no entry”.)

content:
Module 6 > Inventory Purchases, Sales, Returns and Discounts

6-4A –Inventory Sales and Returns
The following transactions occurred for Smithco – a plumbing retailer:
January 6 Sold $3,000 of inventory on account. The inventory cost $1,200. Terms 2/10, n/30.
January 11 Inventory was returned (it was not broken or damaged, just the wrong item.) A $100 credit was
applied to the customer’s account. The returned inventory had a cost of $40.
January 15 Received payment for the amount owing.
Smithco uses a perpetual inventory system.
Required:
a.) Prepare journal entries based on the transactions above.
b.) Redo the January 11 assuming the inventory was returned in bad condition, and needed to be scrapped.
Assume that a full credit of $100 was granted, but the returned inventory had $0 of value.

Julie’s Plumbing Supplies
Trial Balance
31-Mar-29
Debit Credit
Cash $24,000

Inventory 54,000

Module 7 > Cost of Inventory: FIFO, LIFO, Weighted Average, and Specific Identification

7-3A – Lower of Cost and Market Rule (Lower of Cost and Net Realizable Value)
On May 3, 2029, Smith Computing (a computer retailer) purchases a tablet computer for $300, and immediately
puts the tablet on sale for $449 (this tablet computer is INVENTORY, and is not being used by store
employees). The tablet received poor reviews in the press, and it does not sell. By December 31, 2029 the
tablet had been discounted down to $250 and had still not sold.
The company’s fiscal year end is December 31.
On January 14, 2030, the tablet is marked down to $175 and immediately sells.
Required
Record all journal entries and adjusting entries for this tablet computer.
Members Video Walkthrough (Ad Free): https://youtu.be/2hWsdgp47ko
7-3B – Lower of Cost and Market Rule (Lower of Cost and Net Realizable Value)
On June 1, 2029, Bill’s Ski World buys a new pair of Cambria Carbon Pro skis (these skis are inventory) for
$600 and puts them on sale for $999. The skis do not sell, and as of December 31, 2029 they are priced at $500
(this a permanent price drop).
The company’s fiscal year end is December 31.
The skis are marked down on “Final Clearance” on April 6, 2030 for $350 and they sell for that price on April
6.
Required
Record all entries and adjustments required for the life of the skis.
Members Video Walkthrough (Ad Free): https://youtu.be/YzEZiPnRNSY
